You are generating ONE complete Low-Level-Design interview session for an Uber SDE-2 prep app.

FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure:
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/lld_prompt.md   (the production LLD system prompt — your §1–§9 markdown MUST match its structure, including the "## 3.5 API / System Interface" section between §3 and §4, and the §4.1 rule that EVERY class subsection ends with a COMPLETE fenced class block after its (c) operations table — never a bare table; a method-less value record still gets a fenced class block showing its fields)
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/lld_session_builder.md   (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — use the paths I give you below)

OPERATIONAL FACTS (override anything in those files):
- Workspace (create it): /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld/   with tests/ subdir.
- Run code with: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python  (NOT python3 — python3 lacks pytest here). e.g. `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`.
- Canonical flat file layout (bare names, NO packages): models.py, strategies.py (only if a Strategy is in §5), gateways.py (only if §5 names an external dep / injected Clock), <domain>_service.py (named after the DOMAIN, never service.py/utils.py), main.py, tests/conftest.py (sys.path shim), tests/test_<domain>.py.
- Python 3.11+ idioms ONLY: list[X], dict[K,V], X | None. NO 'from __future__ import annotations'. NO Optional/List/Dict. Imports at TOP of file only.
- ONE domain base exception; one specific subclass per failure mode. NEVER raise bare Exception/RuntimeError/ValueError from your own code. NEVER use a bare 'except:'; catch the narrowest correct type. If you must release a lock/permit/notify on ANY failure path, 'except BaseException:' then re-raise is the correct idiom (a domain-only except would leak on a non-domain error).
- Clean single-threaded CORE service with a no-op concurrency hook (e.g. a @contextmanager _lock() that just yields); a ThreadSafe<Domain>Service subclass overrides ONLY the hook(s). §6/§7 core is lock-free and readable; the thread-safe variant is the §9 "second version". Any slow I/O (notify, channel send) stays OUTSIDE the lock.
- NO '...'/'pass' placeholder bodies anywhere. Every method fully implemented. No dead code (every public method exercised by main.py or a test).
- Inject a Clock (gateways.py) wherever time matters (scheduler, TTL) so tests are deterministic — never call the real wall clock inside core logic without an injection seam.

CONCURRENCY TEST RIGOR (critical): the pytest suite MUST include an N-thread race using threading.Barrier so all threads hit the critical section at one instant, with STRICT post-state assertions — not just a count. Assert the invariant ACTUALLY bound (e.g. winners == K, len(set(winner_ids)) == K, every loser got the specific typed error, and the entity's own state field confirms exactly K committed). A single count assertion is necessary-but-NOT-sufficient: if the work is so fast threads serialise, ADD a tiny hold (time.sleep) inside the critical section and assert the peak concurrency actually reached the limit — otherwise the test passes trivially and proves nothing.

MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right):
- Exactly TWO mermaid blocks: a ```mermaid classDiagram in §4.2 and a ```mermaid sequenceDiagram in §8.
- In sequenceDiagram MESSAGE TEXT (after the colon), NEVER use a semicolon ';' — mermaid treats ';' as a statement separator and the parse FAILS. Write "do A then B", not "A; B". Also avoid other tokens that break parsing in message text.
- Keep participant aliases simple.

MARKDOWN OUTPUT:
- Write the COMPLETE §1–§9 markdown (with the ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan at the very top, and §3.5) to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld/_response.md — the full artifact the candidate reads, NOT abbreviated. §6 code blocks tagged ```python copy. The §6 python blocks must match the disk files (no drift, no '...').
- §7.1 and §7.2 must quote the ACTUAL stdout from running main.py and pytest -q (run them, paste real output). Both must be green.

DO NOT assemble any conversation JSON and DO NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator does that afterwards. Your job: leave a WORKING workspace (tests green) + a complete _response.md.

Before finishing: run `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` (exit 0, all cases print OK) and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q` (fully green). If red, fix the CODE (not the test) and re-run.


=== YOUR ASSIGNED PROBLEM ===
UUID: 56b68468-1a94-427b-a556-a86f12dd402f
TITLE: Design a Vending Machine System
DOMAIN (for <domain>_service.py / test_<domain>.py): vending
PROBLEM STATEMENT (put this in §1 and keep it verbatim): Design a vending machine: insert coins, select a product, dispense the product with correct change, refund on cancel. The machine moves through states (IDLE → COLLECTING_MONEY → DISPENSING). Reject invalid selections, insufficient funds, out-of-stock. Thread-safe for a single machine.

DISTINCT CONCURRENCY ANGLE you MUST realize in code + §9 (this is what makes this session unique — do not drift to a generic per-resource lock):
STATE PATTERN (the machine is a finite state machine with explicit state objects/transitions: Idle, HasMoney, Dispensing) + a SINGLE-MACHINE lock making the whole insert→select→dispense transaction atomic (one customer interacts at a time; concurrent button presses must not corrupt the state machine). Patterns: State (primary), Strategy optional (change-making algorithm). Concurrency test: Barrier-sync N threads all trying to buy the LAST item of a 1-stock product; assert exactly ONE succeeds and dispenses, the rest get a typed OutOfStock/typed error and are refunded, and stock ends at 0 — never negative, never double-dispense.

Build the complete session now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/56b68468-1a94-427b-a556-a86f12dd402f_lld/ . Read the two prompt files first, then write code, run tests green with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python, write the full _response.md. Return the structured summary. Set has35=true only if "## 3.5 API / System Interface" is literally in _response.md, and mermaidCount to the actual count of ```mermaid blocks (must be 2).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# LLD Session Builder — Agent Prompt
2	
3	> **Model:** `claude-opus-4-8`
4	> **How to run:** Open Claude Code CLI in your project root, switch model with `/model claude-opus-4-8`, then type the problem statement followed by the contents of this file. OR use the Agent tool from within Claude Code with `model: "opus"` and paste this entire file as the prompt.
5	
6	You are building a **complete, ready-to-open LLD (Low Level Design) interview session** for the Algora frontend. When you are done, the user must be able to open it at `https://localhost:8002/?s=<SESSION_ID>:lld` and see the full design with working code.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## What you must produce
11	
12	Given a problem statement (e.g. "Design a Parking Lot", "Design Splitwise"), you will:
13	
14	1. **Write all Python files** into the workspace directory.
15	2. **Run `main.py`** and capture the actual output.
16	3. **Write the conversation JSON** so the frontend can open the session.
17	
18	---
19	
20	## The LLD System Prompt
21	
22	Before generating anything, read the full LLD system prompt from:
23	```
24	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/lld_prompt.md
25	```
26	This is the exact prompt the AI uses in production. Your output must match every section it describes (§1 through §9), including the ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan at the top.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## Step 1 — Pick a session ID
31	
32	Use a fresh UUID4 for the session. The session ID format is:
33	```
34	<uuid>:lld
35	```
36	Example: `a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:lld`
37	
38	The **slug** (used for file paths) is the session ID with every non-alphanumeric character except `-` and `_` replaced by `_`:
39	```python
40	import re
41	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
42	# "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:lld" → "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890_lld"
43	```
44	
45	---
46	
47	## Step 2 — Workspace structure
48	
49	All Python files go in:
50	```
51	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>/
52	```
53	
54	**CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT — use these EXACT names. Never invent `helpers.py` / `utils.py` / `service.py`.** The service file is named after the DOMAIN (e.g. `locker_service.py`, `parking_service.py`, `booking_service.py`).
55	
56	```
57	models.py             # domain entities, enums, value objects, custom exceptions (the "nouns")
58	strategies.py         # Strategy interfaces + concretes  (ONLY if §5 has a Strategy pattern)
59	gateways.py           # external-system interfaces: PaymentGateway, NotificationSink, Clock  (ONLY if §5 names one)
60	<domain>_service.py   # the orchestrator: clean single-threaded core + ThreadSafe subclass
61	main.py               # small __main__ demo driver: 3-5 cases with print(), NO test framework
62	tests/test_<domain>.py  # pytest suite: every public method, every error path, the concurrency case
63	tests/conftest.py     # adds the parent dir to sys.path so `from models import …` works in tests
64	```
65	
66	Use ~3-6 files total. Python **3.11+** idioms: `list[Foo]`, `X | None` (not `Optional[X]`), no `from __future__ import annotations`.
67	
68	### models.py must contain:
69	- All enums (`class X(IntEnum)` when ordering matters, else `class X(Enum)`)
70	- A single base exception (`<Domain>Error`) and one specific subclass per failure mode — **never mix in a bare `RuntimeError`/`Exception`/`ValueError`**
71	- All frozen dataclasses (`@dataclass(frozen=True)`) for value objects
72	- All mutable dataclasses for entities (the state-machine entity owns its own transition methods)
73	
74	### <domain>_service.py must contain:
75	- The clean single-threaded service with a `@contextmanager _lock()` no-op hook
76	- The `ThreadSafe<Domain>Service(<Domain>Service)` subclass that overrides `_lock()` with the per-key `RLock` double-lock idiom
77	- Every public method from §6, fully implemented (NO `pass`/`...` bodies)
78	- **Imports at the TOP only** — never a mid-file `import threading`
79	- **No dead code** — every method is called from `main.py` or a test
80	
81	### main.py must contain:
82	- `# Python 3.11+` as the first line
83	- A narrated demo: 3-5 cases with `print("Case N … -> OK")`, plain `assert X, message` (no `try/except False` antipattern)
84	
85	### tests/test_<domain>.py must contain (pytest-shaped):
86	- One happy-path test per public operation; one test per typed exception path; a boundary test; an idempotency test if any method is safe-to-call-twice
87	- The **concurrency test** with STRICT assertions: `len(wins) == K`, `len(set(wins)) == K` (unique reservation ids), `len({w.compartment_id …}) == K` (unique resources — catches the silent collision bug), every loss is the typed error, and the count of in-RESERVED/OCCUPIED resources == K. Use `threading.Barrier` so all N threads hit the critical section at one instant. `pytest.raises(<TypedError>)` — never bare `Exception`.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## Step 3 — Generate the full §1–§9 LLD response
92	
93	Using the system prompt from `lld_prompt.md`, generate the complete LLD response as markdown. This is what the user will READ in the frontend — it must be complete, not abbreviated. Match the CURRENT structure exactly:
94	
95	- **⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan** — table (phase | what you do | mode) + the Mode A (whiteboard, no run) vs Mode B (coding env, run) note.
96	- **§1 Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions** — 1.1 plain-words restatement (coffee-chat tone), 1.2 **candidate-LED dialogue** (`**You:**` proposes an assumption/scope-cut + asks to confirm, `**Interviewer:**` ~70% agrees, italic reaction line), 1.3 Final Requirements (numbered functional + "Out of scope" list) + 💬 opener.
97	- **§2 Actors & Core Flows** — bulleted actors + 3-5 one-line happy-path flows. No diagram here.
98	- **§3 Core Entities (accept/reject)** — 3.1 noun walkthrough accepting/**rejecting** each candidate noun with a reason, 3.2 entity table (Entity | Responsibility | Key fields typed | Invariant) + enums block, 3.3 🎙️ spoken-intro paragraph that names the rejected nouns.
99	- **§3.5 API / System Interface** — the orchestrator's **public surface only**, as ONE typed contract table (`Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose`) with 3-7 rows + ONE 🎙️ spoken line on what the surface reveals. Signatures use Python 3.11+ hints and MUST match §4.1 / §4.2 diagram / §6 code one-for-one; `Raises` lists specific typed exceptions (never bare `Exception`). Every method traces to a §1.3 requirement and vice-versa. Comes BEFORE §4 (hellointerview ordering: Requirements → Entities → API → design).
100	- **§4 Class Design** — 4.1 per-class derivation: for EVERY accepted entity (orchestrator first, then each part — entities, value objects, AND strategy interfaces) the FOUR-block format (a) one-line role → (b) state-derivation table **+ a fenced state block** → (c) operations-derivation table **+ a COMPLETE fenced class block (state + constructor + every method from the table)** → (d) design-choice Q&A. ⚠️ Every class subsection MUST end with its own complete fenced class block — never a bare (c) table. Do not let the 2nd/3rd/4th class degrade to table-only after the orchestrator got the full treatment. Then 4.2 consolidated `classDiagram` (draw-vs-say split) + relationships + Pattern Map + 🎙️ box-by-box + **⚠️ PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**.
101	- **§5 Design Patterns & Principles** — 5.1 Information Expert first, 5.2 applied patterns (1-3, each earned, with 💬 layman gloss), 5.3 SOLID tied to named classes, 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule.
102	- **§5.5 Core Algorithms** — A key insight (insight + why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it), B per-operation pseudocode + STORY-driven 🎙️ scripts + a **DRY RUN** of the pipeline method with a state-change table, C data-structure choices (4 cols incl. rejected alternative), D tricky logic (🧠 Claim / 🔬 Proof / 🛠️ Fix), E ONE "❌ what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example, F narration-order table.
103	- **§6 Implementation** — **§6.0 Live Writing Order** (9 phases + 7 golden rules + TL;DR time table + mid-flight situations), then orchestrator SKELETON first, then each method in the 6-block format (role / core-logic bullets / edge-case bullets / code / reasoning PROSE / 🎙️+⚠️), ONE **Approach Comparison** for the trickiest helper, helpers written (not skipped), and a **Verification** subsection with full-lifecycle state-table dry runs.
104	- **§7 Putting It Together** — 7.1 `main.py` narrated demo output, 7.2 `tests/` pytest suite + the actual `pytest -q` output. Both green.
105	- **§8 Key Flow** — a `sequenceDiagram` of the most interesting flow + 🎙️ arrow-by-arrow narration + why-it-shows-the-design-is-good.
106	- **§9 Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility** — 4-step verbal race walkthrough, **CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY table**, **CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A** (why RLock, why not global, deadlock, pessimistic vs optimistic, …), the clean→thread-safe diff (Part A hook+subclass, Part B exact modified method, Part C barrier test proof), edge-cases-handled list, **Extensibility** (level-calibration note + summary table + 2-3 TWIST walkthroughs with minimal code diffs), and the hardest follow-up Q&A (concurrency/distributed/scale — distinct from the twists).
107	
108	**CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE IN §6:**
109	- NO `...` ANYWHERE. No abbreviations. No `# ... rest of implementation`.
110	- Every method body must be complete — every line, every real argument, every real error message, every real return value.
111	- Code blocks tagged ` ```python copy ` so the frontend renders a copy button.
112	- The §6 narration shows code method-by-method (teaching); the FULL files also ship as Write tool blocks (the frontend's LLD "Complete Code Implementation" tabbed viewer renders them from those `write_file` captures).
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Step 4 — Run main.py AND pytest, capture output
117	
118	```bash
119	cd /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>
120	python3 main.py

Three sub-parts: 419 420 ### 1.1 What we're building (plain words) 421 A short paragraph (3-4 sentences MAX) restating the problem in your own conversational words — not a textbook definition, not a feature list. This is the "lead the conversation" pattern — the candidate controls the room. 426 427 Write 5-8 turns. Each turn the candidate proposes a specific assumption + the rationale + asks for confirmation. kill the floor and the spots go with it"). 613 - **Aggregation / association** (open diamond / plain line): who holds a REFERENCE to whom, the multiplicity (1, 0..*, 1..*), and what that reference is FOR at runtime. 614 - **Dependency / uses** (dashed arrow): who transiently calls whom (e.g. the manager USES the factory) without owning it. 615 Make every multiplicity explicit, and call out the ONE relationship that is the extensibility SEAM (usually a Strategy interface the orchestrator depends on). 616 617 After all relationships, add a **Pattern Map** — a small table showing exactly which class embodies which design pattern, so the candidate can answer "which pattern is that?" instantly when the interviewer points at a box: 618 619 | Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason | 620 |---|---|---| 621 | [StrategyInterface] | Strategy | [OrchestratorClass] depends on the abstraction, not the concrete | 622 | [FactoryClass] | Factory Method | translates primitive API inputs into strategy objects | 623 | [ObserverInterface] | Observer | decouple notification from core booking logic | 624 | [Orchestrator]._lock hook | Template Method | no-op in clean core; thread-safe subclass overrides one method | 625 | [Repository] | Repository | separate data access from domain logic | 626 627 Fill this in for the actual classes of THIS problem — do not copy the example headers verbatim. 628 629 Then add a **🎙️ Script: walk the diagram box-by-box.** Write the exact words you'd say while pointing at each box IN THE ORDER you'd draw them: "I put [Orchestrator] in the center because every operation flows through it. Hanging off it on the left is [EntityA] — [one line on what it does]. On the right, [Interface] — this is the Strategy seam, [one line on why it's an interface]. [Concrete1] and [Concrete2] implement it — each is a one-line class. The [Observer] sits below — [one line]. The key seam is [Interface]: the orchestrator never knows which concrete it's talking to, so new [behaviors] plug in as new classes, no core changes." 630 631 ⚠️ **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG — say this immediately after presenting the diagram, before the interviewer asks:** "I notice there's a race condition in [main operation] — if two users call it at the same time, both see 'available', both proceed, we end up double-booking. I'll keep the core design clean and single-threaded for now, and show you exactly how I'd fix that in §9." This single sentence — said proactively, with confidence, before being asked — is the highest-value signal you can give an interviewer. It shows you have built real concurrent systems. Don't wait to be asked about concurrency. Say it right here. 632 633 ## 5. Design Patterns & Principles 634 635 The goal here is NOT to list every pattern you know — it's to name the SMALL number of patterns that actually shape THIS design and explain WHY each one earned its keep. Most LLD interviews need 1-3 patterns max. Listing 6 patterns is a yellow flag — it tells the interviewer you don't know which ones are load-bearing. 636 637 ### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle — always state this first) 638 Before naming any GoF pattern, state the **Information Expert** rationale that drove your class boundaries — i.e. WHO owns WHICH state, and WHY. This is the senior signal: patterns are names; Information Expert is the underlying reasoning. 639 > "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the > data it has. [OrchestratorClass] manages allocation and the lookup map because it's > the only thing that sees all [things]. [EntityA] enforces [its-own-rule] because that > rule is intrinsic to it. [EntityB] owns its physical state because physical presence > is intrinsic to the entity, not relational to the system." 640 This single paragraph is what makes a senior candidate. Without it, the patterns below read as buzzwords. 641 642 ### 5.2 Applied patterns (1-3 of them, each one earned) 643 For EACH PATTERN (only the ones genuinely used in §6 code): 644 - **Name** the pattern 645 - **Why it fits HERE** — the SPECIFIC problem in THIS design that this pattern solves. Not "Strategy is for varying behavior" (textbook); instead "the rule for picking a compartment is exactly the thing the business will want to tweak — fragmentation today, nearest-door tomorrow — so I extract `AllocationStrategy` and the orchestrator never hardcodes the policy." 646 - **Interface + concrete impl** — one line: `AllocationStrategy.select(...) → BestFitAllocation` 647 - **🎙️ Script** — what you say out loud, conversational and confident 648 - **💬 Layman gloss** — one sentence in plain English, no jargon, the way you'd explain to a non-engineer ("It's like the locker doesn't decide which box to use — it asks a rule-of-the-day, so we can swap the rule without rewiring the locker.") 649 650 ### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific, not generic) 651 Don't list "this design respects all of SOLID" — that's empty. Pick the 2-3 principles this design SPECIFICALLY embodies and tie each to a NAMED class: 652 > - **Single Responsibility:** `Compartment` owns physical state, `AllocationStrategy` > owns selection, `LockerService` owns coordination. Three reasons to change, three > classes — never bundled. 653 > - **Open/Closed:** new policy = new `AllocationStrategy` subclass, zero core edits. 654 > - **Dependency Inversion:** `LockerService` depends on the `AllocationStrategy` > abstraction, never on `BestFitAllocation` directly. 655 656 ### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS — the non-negotiable rule 657 This rule applies to every claim in §5.2: 658 - A pattern is "applied" ONLY if its interface/seam is visible in the §6/§7 code as a real interface with a real implementation, a real Observer registration, a real State transition. If the seam isn't in the code, it's not applied. 659 - If something is merely a future extension point, label it **"extension point (not wired in v1)"** — do NOT name-drop it under applied patterns. 660 - Do NOT claim **Singleton** unless you actually enforce a single instance (private constructor, `getInstance`, or DI scope). A class that just happens to have one instance is a **Facade**, not a Singleton — call it that. 661 - Do NOT claim **Factory** for `new Foo(...)`. A Factory is a method/class whose JOB is to translate one type into another (e.g. enum → strategy). If there's no translation, it's just construction. 662 - The interviewer WILL ask you to point at the pattern in the code. Every claim here must survive `Cmd-F`. 663 664 If after applying this rule you have ZERO patterns to claim — that's a valid answer. State it: "I didn't reach for a GoF pattern here; the design is plain Information Expert with three focused classes. If we needed to swap [behavior], I'd introduce a Strategy seam at [point]." That's a stronger answer than name-dropping an unused pattern. 665 666 ## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach 667 THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION FOR THE CANDIDATE — the bridge between the class diagram (WHAT exists) and the code (HOW it's written). Before a single line of code, the candidate must understand the algorithm and data-structure choices that drive the implementation. Cover ALL of the following: 668 669 ### A. The Key Insight (with "why it works" + "what breaks without it") 670 One sentence on the NON-OBVIOUS insight that makes this design clean — then a SHORT follow-up paragraph (3-4 sentences) explaining: - **Why it works** — what property of the problem makes this insight correct - **What would break without it** — the bad version a less-experienced engineer might write, and the specific failure that would surface in production 671 672 This three-part structure is what makes the insight teachable, not just memorable. Example shape: 673 > **Insight:** Treat each compartment as a self-guarding state machine, and make > compartment selection a swappable Strategy. 674 > 675 > **Why it works:** A compartment has a finite, ordered set of legal states > (AVAILABLE → RESERVED → OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE), and the "which compartment to use" > question is a pure policy decision with no shared state. Both naturally factor out: > the entity guards its own transitions, and the strategy returns one box. 676 > 677 > **What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines status writes inside the > orchestrator (`compartment.status = OCCUPIED` directly). Six months in, someone adds > a "RESERVED" state for partial drop-offs, but two methods forget to honor the new > transition rule — and now a compartment can be marked OCCUPIED while still RESERVED. > By making `Compartment` enforce its own transitions, that bug is impossible by > construction. 678 679 The "what breaks without it" paragraph is the senior signal — it shows you've seen the bad version in production. 680 681 ### B. Per-Operation Algorithm (pseudocode + STORY-driven script) 682 For EACH major operation, provide THREE things in order: 683 684 **(1) Pseudocode** — numbered, plain English (NOT Python). Whiteboard-shaped: every guard, every state mutation, every error path explicit. Maps 1-to-1 to §6 code so the candidate can copy line-by-line. 685 686 **(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script** — DO NOT use the same generic "Script — say this" prefix on every operation. Each script must have its OWN character based on the method's STORY: 687 - The **pipeline method** (assign / book / order): structure as "validate → reserve → commit". Open with: "This is the pipeline — three phases." 688 - The **redemption method** (pickup / consume / use): structure as "lookup → guard → release". Open with: "Pickup is three guards and one release. The one-time-ness is in step X." 689 - The **race-prone method**: open by NAMING the race. "This method has the check-then-act race. Steps 4-6 are the critical section — I'll wrap them in §9." 690 - The **cleanup / sweep method**: open with "This runs in the background. Idempotent on purpose — each iteration is independent." 691 - The **lookup method**: open by stating the index it's hitting. "This is an O(1) hit on the `_by_code` map — that's why I built that index in `assign_package`." 692 693 The script MUST reference numbered pseudocode steps explicitly (e.g. "step 4 is where Strategy pays off"). The reader's eye should bounce between the pseudocode and the narration. Generic "this method does X then Y" is BANNED — every script must call out: 694 - The ONE non-obvious step (and WHY it's non-obvious) 695 - The step where a design pattern from §5 visibly fires (`strategy.select`, `observer.notify`, `compartment.reserve`) 696 - The step that handles the edge case the interviewer will probe ("what if the user enters the code at the exact second it expires? — step 2 catches that, look") 697 698 **(3) DRY RUN with concrete inputs** — for the SINGLE most important operation (usually the pipeline method — assign / book / charge), trace through the pseudocode with REAL values. Show the state of the key data structures BEFORE and AFTER each step. This is the strongest interview signal — it proves the algorithm with a tiny example, the way a senior engineer reviews their own code at a whiteboard. Format: 699 700 > **Dry run — `assign_package(locker_id="LKR1", package=Package(id="pkg-1", size=SMALL))`:** 701 > 702 > | Step | Action | State change | 703 > |---|---|---| 704 > | 1 | validate locker exists | locker LKR1 found ✓ | 705 > | 4 | strategy.select(locker, SMALL) | returns Compartment(C7, SMALL, AVAILABLE) | 706 > | 6 | C7.reserve() | C7.status: AVAILABLE → RESERVED | 707 > | 8 | build Reservation | code="9421", expires=now+72h | 708 > | 9 | index it | `_by_code["9421"] = res`, `_reservations[res.id] = res` | 709 > | 11 | notify ASSIGNED | observer fires with `{user, code, expires_at}` | 710 > | 12 | return res | caller receives Reservation with code "9421" | 711 > 712 > *Edge probe: what if a second agent calls `assign_package` while step 6 is mid-flight?* > *Without a lock both see C7 as AVAILABLE — that's the race I flagged. §9 fixes it with > a per-locker RLock around steps 4-9.* 713 714 Only the ONE pipeline method needs a dry run. For pickup / sweep / lookup, the pseudocode + script is enough. 715 716 **Coverage requirement:** every method that appears in the §4 class diagram and §6 code must appear in §5.5 B as pseudocode. Don't skip "boring" methods — if it's in the code, it's in §5.5. 717 718 ### C. Data Structure Choices — with rejected alternatives 719 For EVERY significant data structure, explain why THIS over WHAT-ELSE. The table needs FOUR columns, not three: 720 721 | Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity | 722 |---|---|---|---| 723 | `dict[id → Reservation]` | orchestrator registry | sweep needs O(1) random access by id; a list would be O(n) per cancel | O(1) get/set, O(n) sweep | 724 | `dict[code → Reservation]` | pickup by code | code is the natural key; alternative was scanning reservations on every pickup — O(n) per call → O(1) | O(1) | 725 | linear scan of compartments per locker | best-fit select | n ≤ ~50 boxes/station; per-size heap adds complexity for zero measurable win at this scale | O(n), n small | 726 727 The "vs alternative" column is what shows judgment. "dict because O(1)" is NOT enough — that's a textbook answer. "dict because scanning a list of 10K active reservations on every pickup would be O(n) and pickup is in the hot path" — THAT is a senior answer. 728 729 If you chose a SIMPLER structure over a fancier one (list scan over heap), explicitly SAY SO and give the threshold where you'd flip: "I'd switch to a per-size heap when a station crosses ~500 compartments — the scan becomes a measurable hot spot at that scale." 730 731 ### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix 732 Don't list "tricky things" as random bullets. Each entry has THREE labelled parts so the candidate can defend it under interview pressure: 733 734 **Format for each:** 735 > **🧠 Claim:** [what the tricky thing is, in one sentence] > **🔬 Proof:** [the SPECIFIC sequence of events that exposes the bug if you do it wrong] > **🛠️ Fix in code:** [the exact line / mechanism in §6 that handles it] 736 737 Example: 738 > **🧠 Claim:** `release()` must be idempotent — calling it on an already-AVAILABLE > compartment must be a no-op, not an error. 739 > 740 > **🔬 Proof:** The expiry sweep and a real pickup can race. Both decide the reservation > is done; one calls `release()` first and flips the box AVAILABLE; the second arrives > and would normally hit "illegal transition AVAILABLE → AVAILABLE" — except it'd > wrongly flag a fault on the legitimate pickup path. 741 > 742 > **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Compartment.release()` checks `if self.status in (RESERVED, > OCCUPIED): self.status = AVAILABLE` — outside that set, it returns silently. The > 50-thread test in §7/§9 exercises this exact race. 743 744 Cover 4-6 such tricky points. Race conditions, off-by-one in expiry windows, idempotency, deterministic ordering (why sort by `(size, id)` not just `size`), injected clocks, defensive copies. Each one labelled 🧠 / 🔬 / 🛠️. 745 746 ### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example (one only) 747 Pick the SINGLE step a junior would get wrong, write the wrong version in 2-3 lines of pseudocode, and explain in one sentence WHY it's wrong. This shows you've internalised the failure mode, not just the success path. Example: 748 749 > **❌ Naive version a junior would write:** 750 > ``` 751 > def assign_package(self, locker_id, package): 752 > compartment = self.find_available(locker_id, package.size) # bare lookup 753 > compartment.status = CompartmentStatus.RESERVED # direct write 754 > return Reservation(...) 755 > ``` 756 > **Why it's wrong:** Two failure modes. (1) Direct status write bypasses the state > machine — illegal transitions become possible. (2) The find-then-write is check-then-act > — under concurrency, two callers can both see "available" and both reserve. The > correct version delegates to a Strategy, calls `compartment.reserve()` (so the entity > guards itself), and lifts the whole block under a per-locker lock in §9. 757 758 ONE anti-example for the most critical method. More than one becomes noise. 759 760 ### F. Narration Order for §6 (with method-level open lines) 761 State the exact order in which to present classes to the interviewer, and write the ONE sentence that opens each. The 🎙️ open lines in §6 should literally come from here — don't write them twice. Format: 762 763 | Order | Class | One-line open (literal — say this out loud as you start it) | 764 |---|---|---| 765 | 1 | Enums | "Let me start with the vocabulary — every state in the system, in one place." | 766 | 2 | `Compartment` (state machine) | "This is the heart — the only class allowed to change a compartment's status." | 767 | 3 | `AllocationStrategy` interface + `BestFitAllocation` | "The seam — the orchestrator never knows which policy is wired in." | 768 | 4 | `LockerService.__init__` + skeleton | "Here's the brain. Two indexes — by id for the sweep, by code for O(1) pickup." | 769 | 5 | `LockerService.assign_package` | "Now the interesting part — this is the pipeline I dry-ran above." | 770 | 6 | `LockerService.pickup_package` | "Pickup is three guards and a release. The one-time-ness is the pop on the code map." | 771 772 The open lines here become the literal `🎙️ Script` headers in §6 — never reword them. This eliminates the "every script sounds the same" problem because §5.5.F authors them ONCE, in a varied voice, and §6 just reuses them. 773 774 ## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class 775 Write the BEST version of this code you can — production-quality, not interview-sloppy: clean and idiomatic, FULLY TYPE-HINTED (Python 3.11+ syntax: `list[Foo]`, `X | None`), small cohesive classes, precise names, a short docstring per class, **no dead code**, specific error types (every exception extends a single domain base — never bare `Exception`/`RuntimeError`/`ValueError` mixed in), and the design patterns from §5 VISIBLY wired in (a real Strategy interface with implementations, a real factory, etc.). Keep it genuinely MODULAR per using the CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT — `models.py`, `strategies.py` (if needed), `gateways.py` (if needed), `_service.py`, `main.py`, plus `tests/test_.py`. Never invent new filenames. This is the CLEAN, single-threaded-correct CORE — do NOT add any locks or thread-safety here; concurrency hardening is a deliberate SECOND version in §9. Keep §6/§7 lock-free and readable so the OO design is the star; §7's run proves FUNCTIONAL correctness only. 776 777 PRESENTATION — METHOD BY METHOD, not class by class. Inside the orchestrator, do NOT dump the whole class as one ```python block. Instead, for the orchestrator: 778 1. First show `__init__` + the method signatures (skeleton, with `...` bodies for the methods you'll fill in below). This is the API tour — interviewer sees the full surface area in one glance. 779 2. Then for EACH non-trivial method, present its OWN small ```python block followed by the 4-block format below (role / code / 🎙️ narration / ⚠️ follow-up). 780 781 The skeleton-then-flesh approach matches how the candidate would actually write the class at a whiteboard, and it makes the rendered output scannable instead of one wall of code. Small entity classes (`Compartment`, `Package`, `Reservation`, `Locker`) can still be presented as a single ```python block per file because their methods are short and tightly coupled — the per-method split is only required for the orchestrator. 782 783 **LIVE WRITING ORDER — the candidate's playbook for the live interview itself.** 784 785 This is the BIG mental-model section. The candidate cannot write the §6 code in any order they want — there's a specific phased sequence that maximises interviewer signal and minimises wasted time. ALWAYS render this as a sub-section titled "### 6.0 Live Writing Order (your interview playbook)" BEFORE any code blocks. The candidate reads this OUT LOUD pacing in their head while writing — it's their clock. Total target: ~30 minutes of code time inside a 60-minute interview. 786 787 The section has three parts in order: (a) the 9 phases with what-to-say + what-to-write, (b) the 7 golden rules, (c) a TL;DR time budget table. All three are mandatory. 788 789 #### (a) The 9 phases 790 791 Present each phase as: **Phase title + time budget** → bold "Bolo first" line (what to SAY) → then "Likho:" with a tiny representative code snippet (sub-5-line) so the candidate knows what to write at that step. The snippets are illustrative TEMPLATES adapted to THIS problem (Locker / ParkingLot / etc.) — not full re-renders of §6 code. 792 793 **Phase 1 — Set the stage (1-2 min). NO CODE YET.** 794 > Bolo first: "Main `.py`, `.py`, aur `.py` — ye 3 > files banaunga. Pehle states define karunga (enums), phir `` > ki state machine, phir orchestrator. Code likhne se pehle main aapko har class ka > role bata deta hoon." 795 > Why this matters: 30 seconds of framing tells the interviewer you're not in > chaos. Roadmap pehle, code baad mein. 796 797 **Phase 2 — Enums first (2 min). Quick win.** 798 > Bolo while writing: "States pehle — har class ki vocabulary yahaan se aati hai. > `IntEnum` use kar raha hoon size ke liye taaki 'fits' check ek line ho jaaye." 799 > Likho: 1 small ```python block with the IntEnum + 1-2 plain Enums. No methods. 800 > Why this matters: psychological — first green checkmark on the board, momentum > established. 801 802 **Phase 3 — Most-interesting entity state machine (5 min).** 803 > Bolo: "Ye system ka heart hai. Sirf yeh class apna status badal sakti hai — > orchestrator kabhi seedha status set nahi karega. Isse illegal jumps impossible > ho jaate hain by construction." 804 > Likho: the entity dataclass + every state-transition method (`reserve`, `release`, > `occupy` for Locker; `hold`, `book`, `cancel` for Seat; etc.). Idempotent guards > shown explicitly. 805 > Then immediately drop the **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**: "Yahan ek race > condition hogi `` mein — main usko §9 mein per-resource lock se > fix karunga. Abhi clean version likhta hoon." This single sentence is the > biggest senior signal in the whole interview. 806 807 **Phase 4 — Strategy interface + 1 concrete (1-2 min).** 808 > Bolo: "Strategy seam — orchestrator ko nahi pata best-fit hai ya nearest-door." 809 > Likho: ABC + abstractmethod + 1 concrete impl (~6 lines). 810 > If the design has no Strategy pattern, SKIP this phase entirely — don't fabricate one. 811 812 **Phase 5 — Orchestrator SKELETON (2-3 min).** ⚠️ CRITICAL. 813 > Bolo: "Pehle main saari API surface dikha deta hoon. Phir do main methods bharta hoon." 814 > Likho: ONLY `__init__` (with all fields, all injected dependencies) + EVERY public > method signature with `pass` body. Do NOT fill any body yet. 815 > Why this matters: interviewer sees the full API in one glance. If the candidate > dives straight into `assign_package`'s body, the interviewer waits anxiously > wondering what other methods exist. Skeleton answers that question once and for all. 816 817 **Phase 6 — Most important method, FULL body (5-7 min). Slow down here.** 818 > Bolo step-by-step (this is the most important 5 min of the interview). Walk > through guard clauses → critical block → notify-outside-lock → return. 819 > Likho: the full body of the orchestrator's pipeline method (`assign_package`, > `park`, `bookSeats`, `addExpense`). Include the no-op `_lock` context manager > marking the §9 boundary. Notify call OUTSIDE the lock. 820 > Narration must call out: "Notify lock ke BAAHAR hai — agar SMS slow hai to lock > mat block karo." This one sentence is a senior signal. 821 822 **Phase 7 — Second method, FULL body (3-4 min).** 823 > Bolo: "Ye redemption / completion path hai. guards, phir release." 824 > Likho: full body of `pickup_package` / `unpark` / `settle` etc. Reference §5.5.B > pseudocode 1:1 — no improvisation, just translation. 825 826 **Phase 8 — Demo verbally OR run (3-4 min).** 827 > Mode A (Amazon-style, no execution): "Main 3 cases verbally walk karunga: > happy path, capacity-full, edge case." Then SHOW state transitions in plain > text on the board: 828 > ``` 829 > assign(SMALL pkg) → C1: AVAILABLE → RESERVED, _by_code={"9421": res-1} 830 > deposit(res-1) → C1: RESERVED → OCCUPIED 831 > pickup("9421") → C1: OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE, _by_code={} 832 > ``` 833 > Mode B (Uber/Meta coding env): `python3 main.py`, show actual stdout. 834 > Wait until ALL methods are full-body before this step. A demo with one half-written > method is worse than no demo. 835 836 **Phase 9 — Concurrency (2-3 min, ONLY if asked).** 837 > Bolo first, ALWAYS: "Race `` mein hai — do callers same resource > dekh ke dono reserve kar dete hain. Fix: per-resource RLock through Template > Method — `_lock` hook ko subclass override karta hai, core code zero changes." 838 > Likho the thread-safe subclass ONLY if interviewer says "show me the code". > Otherwise stay verbal. 839 840 #### (b) The 7 golden rules — must be rendered as a numbered list 841 842 1. **Roadmap pehle bolo.** "Main 3 files banaunga, A → B → C." 30 seconds of talking saves the interviewer's anxiety and frames everything that follows. 843 844 2. **Skeleton-then-flesh.** Orchestrator gets `pass` bodies first, full bodies later. Never write the orchestrator class as one ```python blob. 845 846 3. **State machine before orchestrator.** Compartment / Seat / Spot — write these first. They're independent; orchestrator imports them. 847 848 4. **BOLO while likhing.** Silence = interviewer's brain wanders. Even a one-liner helps: "yahan clock inject kar raha hoon", "yeh guard rejection path hai". 849 850 5. **Concurrency flag drop karo entity ke baad.** Proactively, before the interviewer asks. Single biggest senior signal of the whole hour. 851 852 6. **Demo wait karo.** Even one half-written method = no demo. Show progress only once everything runs. 853 854 7. **Pseudocode → code 1:1.** §5.5.B's numbered logic becomes §6's code in the SAME order. Don't improvise live — translate the dry-run. 855 856 #### (c) TL;DR time budget table — render exactly this shape 857 858 | Time | Phase | What you're writing | 859 |---|---|---| 860 | 0-2 min | Roadmap | Just talking, no code | 861 | 2-4 min | Enums | Full enums | 862 | 4-9 min | State-machine entity | Full state machine class + methods | 863 | 9-11 min | Strategy seam | ABC + 1 concrete (skip if no Strategy in §5) | 864 | 11-14 min | Orchestrator skeleton | `__init__` + signatures with `pass` | 865 | 14-21 min | Pipeline method (`assign` / `park` / `book`) | Full body + narration | 866 | 21-25 min | Redemption method (`pickup` / `unpark` / `settle`) | Full body + narration | 867 | 25-29 min | Demo / verbal trace | 3 cases | 868 | 29-32 min | Concurrency answer | Verbal first, code only if asked | 869 870 Total: ~30 minutes of code time. In a 60-min interview, allocate 25 min for §1-§5 (talking + diagram), 30 min for §6 code, 5 min for follow-ups. 871 872 #### (d) Mid-flight situations — when things don't go to plan 873 874 Every interview hits at least one of these. The candidate must know the response before the situation arises — improvising under pressure usually makes it worse. Render this as a numbered list of 5 situations, each with a one-line response.

content.text: I have read the first part of the lld_prompt.md (lines 1-874). Let me read the rest to understand §7, §8, §9 structure.

content.content:
875	
876	  1. **Interviewer interrupts mid-method.** STOP writing. Don't try to talk and write simultaneously — both will suffer. Address the question fully, then say "OK, where was I — I was in the middle of `<method>`, let me finish the lock block." Resume from the same line. Common mistake: keeping the cursor moving while answering → you'll write something nonsensical.
877	
878	  2. **Running out of time at the 40-min mark.** Cut in this EXACT order, never skip an earlier item to preserve a later one:
879	  > - **First cut:** second method body — describe verbally instead. "I'll describe `<second_method>` instead of writing it — same shape, three guards then release."
880	  > - **Second cut:** §6 demo / verbal trace — skip if you've narrated dry-runs in §5.5.
881	  > - **Third cut:** §9 code — stay verbal, never write the thread-safe subclass under time pressure.
882	  > - **NEVER cut:** state machine, pipeline method body, proactive concurrency flag. These three ARE the senior signal — losing any one costs more than overrunning by 2-3 min.

1031	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1032	
1033	**DEFAULT: DESCRIBE the solution. Write code only if the interviewer explicitly asks.**
1034	
1035	In a real interview, after presenting §6 code, the interviewer will probe: "What about concurrent users?" Your answer should be verbal first — clear, confident, specific. Only write the thread-safe version if they say "show me the code" or "implement it."
1036	
1037	**DESCRIBE THIS VERBALLY (always):**
1038	
1039	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race condition:**
1040	"The problem is in [method name]. Two users call it at the same time. Both check — both see 'available'. Both proceed. Both succeed. We have double-booking. This is called a check-then-act problem — the check and the action are not one atomic step."
1041	
1042	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:**
1043	"I'd add a lock to the [OrchestratorClass]. Before [method] starts checking, it acquires the lock. While it holds the lock, no other thread can enter. After the check-and-assign completes, it releases the lock. Now both users race to acquire the lock — only one wins, the other waits, then sees the resource is already taken."
1044	
1045	🗣️ **Step 3 — Name the trade-off:**
1046	"The coarse lock is simple and correct. The downside: only one [operation] can happen at a time. If we need higher throughput, we'd move to per-resource locking — each [resource] has its own lock, so ten different users booking ten different [resources] don't block each other."
1047	
1048	🗣️ **Step 4 — Address the distributed case (if asked):**
1049	"For multiple servers, an in-process lock is not enough — each server has its own memory, so my Python lock only protects ONE process. We'd push the atomicity down to the database with an optimistic lock — 'mark this resource as held, but ONLY if it's still available' — one SQL write that checks and updates in a single atomic step: `UPDATE compartment SET status='RESERVED' WHERE id=? AND status='AVAILABLE'`. Whoever's update affects 0 rows lost the race and retries or gets a clean error."
1050	
1051	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY — be fluent in these (the candidate must be able to define each in plain words AND point to where it appears in THIS design).** An interviewer scores concurrency on whether you USE the right words correctly, not just whether the code works. Provide a compact table mapping each term to a plain definition + where it bites in this specific problem:
1052	
1053	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1054	|---|---|---|
1055	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once and the result depends on timing | `select` + `reserve` in `assign_package` |
1056	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the select-reserve-index block |
1057	| **Check-then-act** | You check a condition, then act on it — but state changed in between | "saw AVAILABLE, then reserved" — the classic bug |
1058	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing — no other thread can observe a half-done state | what the lock guarantees for the critical section |
1059	| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.Lock` / `RLock` on the locker |
1060	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it already holds | `pickup` holds the lock and calls `_expire`, which re-takes it |
1061	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse (one global) vs fine (per-resource) | we chose per-LOCKER, not one global lock |
1062	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock BEFORE touching state (assume conflict) | the in-process `RLock` approach |
1063	| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry if you lost (assume no conflict) | the DB compare-and-set for the distributed case |
1064	| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | Atomic "update only if value is still X" | the `WHERE status='AVAILABLE'` SQL clause |
1065	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs — both wait forever | avoided here: we only ever hold ONE lock at a time |
1066	| **Starvation** | A thread never gets the lock because others keep winning | not a risk here; locks are short-held |
1067	
1068	The candidate reads this and can DROP these terms naturally: "this is a check-then-act race in the critical section; I take a pessimistic per-locker mutex — reentrant because `pickup` re-enters via `_expire` — and for the distributed case I switch to optimistic locking with a compare-and-set." That single sentence signals senior-level fluency.

1070	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A — the probing questions after your basic answer.** Interviewers rarely stop at "add a lock." They probe. Give the EXACT spoken answer to each — 4-6 of these, chosen for THIS problem:
1071	
1072	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?" > "Because a method that holds the lock calls another method that ALSO takes the lock — > `pickup` holds it and calls `_expire`, which re-acquires it. A plain `Lock` would > deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` (reentrant lock) lets > the owning thread re-enter, and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."
1073	
1074	> ❓ "Why not just lock the whole method, or use one global lock?" > "One global lock is correct but kills throughput — every station serializes through > one bottleneck even though they share no state. Per-LOCKER locking lets ten different > stations book in parallel; only agents at the SAME station serialize, and that's > microseconds. Locking the whole method also needlessly holds the lock during the > notify call — I keep `notify` OUTSIDE the lock so a slow SMS never blocks other agents."
1075	
1076	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?" > "Deadlock needs two locks acquired in different orders. Here I only ever hold ONE lock > at a time — the per-locker lock — and the notify/IO happens outside it. 1077	
1078	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which did you pick and why?" > "Pessimistic in-process because the critical section is tiny and contention at one > station is low — grabbing a short-held lock is simpler and has no retry loop. For the > DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic: a DB compare-and-set, because you can't hold a > Python lock across servers, and at that layer a conditional write + retry is the > idiomatic atomic primitive."
1079	
1080	> ❓ "This is read-heavy (lots of pickups, few deposits) — would a read-write lock help?" > "If reads dominated and didn't mutate, a read-write lock would let many readers proceed > in parallel and only block on writes. But here even `pickup` MUTATES (it frees the box > and burns the code), so it's not a pure read — a read-write lock wouldn't buy much. > I'd reach for it only if I had a genuinely read-only query path."
1081	
1082	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?" > "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so the half-done state is gone with > it — there's no orphaned lock to clean up. # Python 3.11+
"""
vending_service.py — the orchestrator (the "brain") + the thread-safe variant.

VendingService is the public API a customer (or a UI) talks to. It owns the
machine's working memory — the coins inserted so far, the chosen slot, the
catalog of slots, the coin bank — and it FORWARDS each button press to the
current state object, then adopts whatever state that object returns.

🎙️ "The service is deliberately thin. It does no business logic itself — it
    asks the current state to handle the action and swap itself out. That keeps
    the state-machine rules in the state classes where they belong. The service's
    only job is to hold the data and route the call."

CONCURRENCY MODEL (one model, applied consistently):
    A single vending machine is ONE shared object that many button-press threads
    can hit at once. The whole insert -> select -> dispense -> cancel transaction
    must be atomic — one customer interacts at a time. So we guard the WHOLE
    machine with ONE lock (the machine IS the unit of contention; there is no
    per-slot parallelism to win — they share the inserted-coins tally and the
    coin bank). The clean core below has a no-op _lock() hook; the ThreadSafe
    subclass overrides ONLY that hook with a real RLock. §6/§7 stay lock-free.
"""

import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    Coin,
    CoinBank,
    DispenseResult,
    IdleState,
    MachineState,
    MachineStateName,
    Slot,
    VendingContext,
)


class VendingService(VendingContext):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The single entry point. It implements VendingContext so the State classes
    # can read/mutate its working memory without the service leaking its guts.
    # 🎙️ "Every customer action — insert, select, dispense, cancel — comes in
    #     here, gets wrapped in the (no-op) lock, and is delegated to the current
    #     state. The state returns the next state; we just store it."

    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
        self._slots = slots
        self._coin_bank = coin_bank
        self._inserted: list[Coin] = []
        self._selected_code: str | None = None
        # The machine always boots in IDLE.
        self._state: MachineState = IdleState()

    # ── VendingContext implementation (what the State classes see) ────────────
    @property
    def slots(self) -> dict[str, Slot]:
        return self._slots

    @property
    def coin_bank(self) -> CoinBank:
        return self._coin_bank

    @property
    def inserted(self) -> list[Coin]:
        return self._inserted

    @property
    def selected_code(self) -> str | None:
        return self._selected_code

    @selected_code.setter
    def selected_code(self, value: str | None) -> None:
        self._selected_code = value

    # ── the concurrency hook (Template Method) ───────────────────────────────
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there is zero lock code
        #      in this file's hot path. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY
        #      this one method with a real RLock. The diff is ~5 lines.
        yield

    # ── public API ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def insert_coin(self, coin: Coin) -> int:
        # WHY: forward to the current state; it decides the next state. Returns
        #      the running total inserted so the UI/caller can show a balance.
        with self._lock():
            self._state = self._state.insert_coin(self, coin)
            return self.inserted_cents()

    def select_product(self, code: str) -> None:
        # WHY: validate-and-remember the chosen slot. The state guards which
        #      transitions are legal; unknown/out-of-stock codes raise typed errors.
        with self._lock():
            self._state = self._state.select(self, code)

    def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult:
        # WHY: the money-shot. The whole check-funds -> make-change -> vend block
        #      runs as ONE atomic step under the lock so two threads can't both
        #      vend the last unit. On any failure a typed error bubbles up and the
        #      customer's money stays refundable.
        with self._lock():
            self._state, result = self._state.dispense(self)
            return result

    def cancel(self) -> tuple[Coin, ...]:
        # WHY: refund everything inserted and reset to IDLE. Returns the coins so
        #      the caller can physically return them.
        with self._lock():
            self._state, refund = self._state.cancel(self)
            return refund

    def refill(self, code: str, units: int) -> None:
        # WHY: operator action. Delegates to the slot, which guards positive units.
        with self._lock():
            slot = self._slots.get(code)
            if slot is None:
                from models import InvalidSelectionError

                raise InvalidSelectionError(f"no such product code: {code}")
            slot.refill(units)

    # ── read-only inspection (handy for the demo and tests) ───────────────────
    @property
    def state_name(self) -> MachineStateName:
        return self._state.name

    def balance_cents(self) -> int:
        return self.inserted_cents()


class ThreadSafeVendingService(VendingService):
    # ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The §9 "second version". It changes NOTHING about the business logic — it
    # overrides exactly ONE method, _lock(), to take a real reentrant lock around
    # every public operation.
    #
    # Why ONE lock for the whole machine (not per-slot)?
    #   A single machine has ONE coin slot and ONE inserted-coins tally that every
    #   action shares. There's no "two customers buy two different slots in
    #   parallel" win to chase — they'd still fight over the same inserted-money
    #   and the same coin bank. So the machine itself is the unit of mutual
    #   exclusion: one customer's full transaction at a time.
    #
    # Why RLock (reentrant) not a plain Lock?
    #   A public method holding the lock may call another guarded path on the same
    #   thread; an RLock lets the owning thread re-enter without deadlocking.
    #
    # 🎙️ "The thread-safe machine is a five-line subclass: I override _lock to
    #     wrap each operation in a single reentrant lock. The state classes and the
    #     service body never change — that's the Template Method hook paying off."

    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
        super().__init__(slots, coin_bank)
        self._machine_lock = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._machine_lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""
main.py — a narrated demo driver for the vending machine.

Run it:  python main.py
It walks 6 cases: happy path with change, exact money, insufficient funds,
invalid selection, out of stock, and cancel-with-refund. Each prints "-> OK".
This is for DEMONSTRATION; rigorous coverage lives in tests/.
"""

from models import (
    Coin,
    CoinBank,
    InsufficientFundsError,
    InvalidSelectionError,
    OutOfStockError,
    Product,
    Slot,
)
from vending_service import VendingService


def build_machine() -> VendingService:
    # A small catalog: A1 cola (75c), A2 water (100c), A3 gum (5c, last one).
    slots = {
        "A1": Slot(Product("A1", "Cola", 75), quantity=3),
        "A2": Slot(Product("A2", "Water", 100), quantity=2),
        "A3": Slot(Product("A3", "Gum", 5), quantity=1),
    }
    # Coin bank pre-loaded with change to give back.
    bank = CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 10, Coin.DIME: 10, Coin.NICKEL: 10, Coin.PENNY: 20})
    return VendingService(slots, bank)


def case_happy_path_with_change() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    # Insert $1.00 for a 75c cola -> expect 25c change.
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.select_product("A1")
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert result.product.name == "Cola", result
    assert result.change_cents == 25, result.change
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE", svc.state_name
    print(f"Case 1 buy cola with $1.00, get 25c change -> OK  ({result.change})")


def case_exact_money_no_change() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    svc.select_product("A2")  # water is exactly 100c
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert result.change_cents == 0, result.change
    print("Case 2 buy water with exact $1.00, no change -> OK")


def case_insufficient_funds() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)  # only 25c toward a 75c cola
    svc.select_product("A1")
    try:
        svc.dispense()
        assert False, "expected InsufficientFundsError"
    except InsufficientFundsError as exc:
        assert "only 25" in str(exc), exc
    # Money is still refundable.
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert sum(c.cents for c in refund) == 25, refund
    print("Case 3 insufficient funds rejected, then refunded 25c -> OK")


def case_invalid_selection() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    try:
        svc.select_product("Z9")
        assert False, "expected InvalidSelectionError"
    except InvalidSelectionError as exc:
        assert "Z9" in str(exc), exc
    print("Case 4 invalid slot code rejected -> OK")


def case_out_of_stock() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    # Buy the single gum first.
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    svc.select_product("A3")
    svc.dispense()
    # Now the slot is empty — a second buyer is rejected.
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    try:
        svc.select_product("A3")
        assert False, "expected OutOfStockError"
    except OutOfStockError as exc:
        assert "A3" in str(exc), exc
    svc.cancel()
    print("Case 5 last gum sold, second buyer gets OutOfStock -> OK")


def case_cancel_refunds_all() -> None:
    svc = build_machine()
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DIME)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert sum(c.cents for c in refund) == 40, refund
    assert svc.balance_cents() == 0
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE"
    print("Case 6 cancel refunds all 40c and returns to IDLE -> OK")


def main() -> None:
    print("=== Vending Machine demo ===")
    case_happy_path_with_change()
    case_exact_money_no_change()
    case_insufficient_funds()
    case_invalid_selection()
    case_out_of_stock()
    case_cancel_refunds_all()
    print("All cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Python 3.11+
"""
test_vending.py — pytest suite for the vending machine.

Covers every public method, every typed error path, boundary cases, and the
concurrency race: N threads all try to buy the LAST unit of a 1-stock product
at the same instant. Exactly ONE wins and dispenses; the rest get OutOfStock
and are refunded; stock ends at 0 — never negative, never double-dispense.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from models import (
    CannotMakeChangeError,
    Coin,
    CoinBank,
    DispenseResult,
    InsufficientFundsError,
    InvalidSelectionError,
    InvalidStateError,
    OutOfStockError,
    Product,
    Slot,
)
from vending_service import ThreadSafeVendingService, VendingService


def make_slots() -> dict[str, Slot]:
    return {
        "A1": Slot(Product("A1", "Cola", 75), quantity=3),
        "A2": Slot(Product("A2", "Water", 100), quantity=2),
        "A3": Slot(Product("A3", "Gum", 5), quantity=1),
    }


def make_bank() -> CoinBank:
    return CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 10, Coin.DIME: 10, Coin.NICKEL: 10, Coin.PENNY: 20})


@pytest.fixture
def svc() -> VendingService:
    return VendingService(make_slots(), make_bank())


# ── happy paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def test_buy_with_change(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    for _ in range(4):
        svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)  # $1.00
    svc.select_product("A1")
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert isinstance(result, DispenseResult)
    assert result.product.name == "Cola"
    assert result.change_cents == 25
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE"
    assert svc.slots["A1"].quantity == 2


def test_buy_exact_money_no_change(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    svc.select_product("A2")
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert result.change == ()
    assert result.change_cents == 0


def test_select_before_paying_then_pay(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    # Pre-select while IDLE, then feed coins.
    svc.select_product("A3")
    assert svc.state_name.value == "COLLECTING_MONEY"
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert result.product.name == "Gum"
    assert result.change_cents == 0


def test_insert_coin_returns_running_total(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    assert svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER) == 25
    assert svc.insert_coin(Coin.DIME) == 35


def test_refill_restocks_slot(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    svc.select_product("A3")
    svc.dispense()  # A3 now 0
    assert svc.slots["A3"].quantity == 0
    svc.refill("A3", 5)
    assert svc.slots["A3"].quantity == 5


# ── typed error paths ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def test_invalid_selection(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidSelectionError):
        svc.select_product("Z9")


def test_insufficient_funds(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)  # 25c for a 75c cola
    svc.select_product("A1")
    with pytest.raises(InsufficientFundsError):
        svc.dispense()
    # Money still refundable.
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert sum(c.cents for c in refund) == 25


def test_out_of_stock_after_last_unit(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    svc.select_product("A3")
    svc.dispense()
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    with pytest.raises(OutOfStockError):
        svc.select_product("A3")


def test_dispense_without_selection_raises(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidStateError):
        svc.dispense()


def test_dispense_while_idle_raises(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidStateError):
        svc.dispense()


def test_refill_unknown_slot_raises(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidSelectionError):
        svc.refill("Z9", 3)


def test_refill_nonpositive_units_raises(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidSelectionError):
        svc.refill("A1", 0)


def test_cannot_make_change_rejects_and_refunds() -> None:
    # Bank holds NO coins, so any non-zero change request fails. Product 70c,
    # pay with a dollar -> owe 30c change the bank can't form.
    slots = {"B1": Slot(Product("B1", "Chips", 70), quantity=1)}
    bank = CoinBank({})  # empty float
    svc = VendingService(slots, bank)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    svc.select_product("B1")
    with pytest.raises(CannotMakeChangeError):
        svc.dispense()
    # Bank must be unchanged (all-or-nothing) and money refundable.
    assert svc.coin_bank.inventory.get(Coin.DOLLAR, 0) == 0
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert sum(c.cents for c in refund) == 100
    # Stock untouched — we never vended.
    assert svc.slots["B1"].quantity == 1


# ── boundary / state machine ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
def test_cancel_refunds_all_and_resets(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DIME)
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert sum(c.cents for c in refund) == 40
    assert svc.balance_cents() == 0
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE"


def test_cancel_while_idle_is_noop(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    refund = svc.cancel()
    assert refund == ()
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE"


def test_reselect_overwrites_choice(svc: VendingService) -> None:
    svc.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    svc.select_product("A1")
    svc.select_product("A2")  # change mind
    result = svc.dispense()
    assert result.product.name == "Water"


def test_make_change_is_all_or_nothing() -> None:
    # 30c from {quarter:1, nickel:1} -> quarter+nickel; bank empties exactly.
    bank = CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 1, Coin.NICKEL: 1})
    change = bank.make_change(30)
    assert sorted(c.cents for c in change) == [5, 25]
    assert bank.inventory[Coin.QUARTER] == 0
    assert bank.inventory[Coin.NICKEL] == 0


def test_make_change_failure_leaves_bank_untouched() -> None:
    bank = CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 1})  # can't form 10c
    before = dict(bank.inventory)
    with pytest.raises(CannotMakeChangeError):
        bank.make_change(10)
    assert bank.inventory == before


# ── concurrency: N threads race for the LAST unit ─────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class Slot:
    product: Product
    quantity: int
    # WHY: an injectable seam (defaults to a no-op). Tests set this to a tiny
    #      time.sleep so the gap between the "is there stock?" check and the
    #      "take one" decrement is WIDE — that's the exact window two unlocked
    #      threads would both slip through. It is the moral equivalent of the
    #      injected Clock: it makes a timing-dependent race reproducible instead
    #      of relying on luck. In production this stays None (zero cost).
    pre_vend_hook: "Callable[[], None] | None" = None

    def is_in_stock(self) -> bool:
        return self.quantity > 0

    def vend_one(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the slot guards its own invariant. If two code paths both tried
        #      to dispense the last unit, this raise is the backstop that makes
        #      "stock can never go negative" true by construction, not by hope.
        if self.quantity <= 0:
            raise OutOfStockError(f"slot {self.product.code} is empty")

        if self.pre_vend_hook is not None:
            # WHY: widen the check-then-act window so an UNLOCKED machine really
            #      races (two threads both pass the check above, both decrement).
            #      Under the real lock this hook runs while holding the lock, so
            #      no second thread can be inside vend_one at the same time.
            self.pre_vend_hook()
        self.quantity -= 1

def test_concurrent_buyers_with_hold_proves_lock_is_load_bearing() -> None:
    # Hardening: a count assertion is necessary but NOT sufficient — under the
    # GIL the vend is so fast that even an UNLOCKED machine usually serializes
    # by luck and passes trivially. So we inject a real hold INSIDE the critical
    # section (the pre_vend_hook), which widens the check-then-act window AND
    # lets us measure the PEAK number of threads simultaneously inside vend_one.
    #
    # Under the real lock that peak MUST be 1 — the lock makes the whole
    # transaction atomic, so no two threads are ever inside the critical section
    # at the same instant. If peak ever exceeded 1, the lock would be a lie.
    N = 30
    slots = {"X1": Slot(Product("X1", "Bar", 25), quantity=1)}
    bank = CoinBank({})
    svc = ThreadSafeVendingService(slots, bank)

    inside = 0
    peak = 0
    meter_lock = threading.Lock()

    def hold() -> None:
        nonlocal inside, peak
        with meter_lock:
            inside += 1
            peak = max(peak, inside)
        time.sleep(0.01)  # hold the critical section open for 10ms
        with meter_lock:
            inside -= 1

    slots["X1"].pre_vend_hook = hold

    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
    wins: list[DispenseResult] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
            svc.select_product("X1")
            result = svc.dispense()
            with guard:
                wins.append(result)
        except OutOfStockError as exc:
            with guard:
                losses.append(exc)
        finally:
            try:
                svc.cancel()
            except Exception:
                pass

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(N)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # The invariant actually bound:
    assert len(wins) == 1, f"expected exactly 1 winner, got {len(wins)}"
    assert wins[0].product.code == "X1"
    assert len(losses) == N - 1
    assert all(isinstance(e, OutOfStockError) for e in losses)
    assert slots["X1"].quantity == 0  # never negative, never double-dispensed
    # PEAK CONCURRENCY PROOF: only the winner ever entered vend_one (others were
    # rejected at the in-stock check before the hook), so peak is exactly 1, and
    # the lock guaranteed they never overlapped.
    assert peak == 1, f"two threads were inside the critical section: peak={peak}"
